  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

20135 - Analysis of CMR data using mrmr

Roland Knapp

Analysis of Ranger Station Pond CMR dataset - Tyndall area - “treatment” covariate is included in translocation file to evaluate survival of Bd-exposed and unexposed frogs

Install mrmr package if current version is not available

remotes::install_github("SNARL1/mrmr")

Install cmdstanr package if current version is not available

install.packages("cmdstanr", repos = c("https://stan-dev.r-universe.dev", getOption("repos")))
cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()

Load packages

if (!require(librarian)){
  install.packages("librarian")
  library(librarian)
}
librarian::shelf(here, cmdstanr, mrmr, bayesplot, posterior, tidyverse)

Create required datasets

captures <- read_csv(here("data", "clean", "20135_capture.csv"))
surveys <- read_csv(here("data", "clean", "20135_survey.csv"))
translocations <- read_csv(here("data", "clean", "20135_reintroduction.csv"))

Specify model formula and check data

data_20135 <- clean_data(captures, surveys, translocations,
                                survival_formula = ~ treatment,
                                survival_fill_value = c(treatment = "unexposed"))

Fit model

# Used default of 1000 iterations for warmup and 1000 iterations for sampling
# "threads per chain" option and "cpp_options" did not speed up fit_model step
model_20135 <- fit_model(
  data_20135, 
  chains = 4,
  parallel_chains = 4,
  threads_per_chain = 4,
  cpp_options = list(stan_threads = TRUE)
)

Save model object

model_20135 %>% write_rds(here("out", "model", "20135_model.rds"), "xz", compression = 9L)

Read model objects back in (as necessary)

model_20135 <- read_rds(here("out", "model", "20135_model.rds"))

Evaluate convergence of the MCMC algorithm

  • alpha_lamda: Baseline (intercept) of the recruitment process. In a Jolly-Seber model, recruitment (entry of new individuals into the population) in each primary period is modeled using a log link or logit link. For instance, if log⁡(λt )=αλ+ϵλ, then alpha_lambda is the overall mean log recruitment rate across time period.
  • sigma_lamda: Standard deviation of temporal random effects on recruitment (ε_λ,t). Therefore, sigma_lamda describes variability in recruitment through time.
  • beta_phi[1]: Intercept (baseline effect) for survival probability (φ). Survival is typically modeled on the logit scale: logit(ϕi,t)=βϕ,1+βϕ,2⋅Treatmenti+ϵϕ,t. beta_phi[1] is the average (logit-scale) survival for the reference group — typically “unexposed”.
  • beta_phi[2]: Treatment effect on survival probability. Continuing from beta_phi[1] above, beta_phi[2] modifies the baseline logit of survival for the treatment group (e.g., Bd-exposed vs. unexposed). Positive versus negative values indicate that the treatment increased or decreased survival, respectively.
  • sigma_phi: Standard deviation of temporal random effects on survival (ε_φ,t). Captures variability in survival between time periods.
  • beta_detect[1]: Intercept (baseline effect) for detection probability (p). Represents the average log-odds of detecting an individual per secondary period, in the baseline group (e.g., unexposed).
# Traceplots
pars_to_plot <- c("alpha_lambda", 
                  "sigma_lambda", 
                  "beta_phi", 
                  "sigma_phi", 
                  "beta_detect")

mcmc_trace(model_20135$m_fit$draws(pars_to_plot))


# Summary statistics, include Rhat (any Rhat > 1.01?)
model_20135$m_fit$summary(pars_to_plot)

Built-in visualizations

plot_model(model_20135, what = "abundance")

plot_model(model_20135, what = "recruitment")

plot_model(model_20135, what = "survival")

Compare posterior estimates for survival: Bd-exposed vs. unexposed individudals

  • rstan saves the survival value as phi. In contrast, cmdstanr stores the components of the survival variable: beta_phi[1] = intercept, beta_phi[2] = treatment_effect, and eps_phi[1:13] = time (primary period) random effects. The following code calculates phi from these components.
cat("Posterior probability that treatment increased survival:", treatment_prob, "\n")

Posterior probability that treatment increased survival: 0.206
  • Posterior probability that treatment increased survival: 0.206. Therefore, there is a 21% posterior probability that the treatment increased survival. Weak evidence at best.

Plot the posterior distribution of the treatment effect

Custom plots of cohort survival by treatment group

  • Plot cohort survival by treatment group, with 95% credible intervals and lines/points on the posterior median.

Arrange survival, abundance, and recruitment plots

# survival plot
p1 <- p1 +
#  guides(fill = guide_legend(nrow = 1)) + 
  theme(legend.position = "right")

# abundance plot
p2 <- plot_model(model_20135, what = "abundance") 
p2 <- p2 + ggtitle("B. Population size") +
    ylab("Number of adults")

# recruitment plot
p3 <- plot_model(model_20135, what = "recruitment")
p3 <- p3 + ggtitle("C. Recruitment") +
    ylab("Number of adults")

library(patchwork) 

p1 / p2 / p3 +
  plot_layout(heights = c(3, 1, 1))

ggsave(here("out", "plots", "20135_mrmr_plots_treatment.png"), width = 6.5, height = 7.2)

Quick model check

  • If the treatment increased survival, then in 2018 and 2019, we should have seen a greater proportion of treated than untreated frogs from the initial translocation.
cohort_transloc <- translocations %>%
  filter(release_date == as.Date("2016-07-18"))

count(cohort_transloc, treatment) %>%
  mutate(frac = n / sum(n))
  
captures %>%
  inner_join(translocations, by = "pit_tag_id") %>% 
  select(survey_date, pit_tag_id, treatment) %>% 
  filter(survey_date > as.Date("2018-01-01"), 
         pit_tag_id %in% cohort_transloc$pit_tag_id) %>%
  group_by(survey_date) %>%
  count(treatment) %>%
  mutate(frac = n / sum(n))
  • Our null expectation is that we should see approximately 71% exposed and 29% unexposed individuals based on the proportions of frogs in each category in this first translocation.
  • For each survey, we see a somewhat larger fraction of exposed individuals than expected based on our null expectation.
